standard_description: Solve real world problems involving multiplication of fractions and mixed numbers, e.g., by using visual fraction models or equations to represent the problem.
subject: Math

problems.text:
In a bag of 80 Skittles, 35% of the candies are orange. How many orange Skittles are in the bag?
Show your answer using two different strategies.

Problem 1
Solve each problem in two different ways.
a.
$${3{1\over3} \times 1{1\over4}}$$
b.
$${5{2\over5} \times 3{4\over9}}$$
Problem 2
Which strategy was more efficient for each problem above? Explain.

Problem 1
Find the value of each of the following.
a.
$${{1\over4}}$$
of
$${{12}}$$
b.
$${{3\over4}}$$
of
$${{12}}$$
Problem 2
There are 21 students in the chess club.
$$2\over3$$
of them are girls. How many girls are in the chess club?

Problem 1
a.   A construction worker is tiling a backsplash. The backsplash is
$${4{{{1\over2}}}}$$
tiles long and
$${{1\over2}}$$
tile tall. How many tiles are needed in total for the whole backsplash? Give an exact answer that includes the fractions of a tile she will need.
b.   A runway measures
$${10{1\over3}}$$
yards long and
$${{3\over4}}$$
yards wide. What is the area, in square yards, of the runway?
Problem 2
a.   Kendra is making
$${{1\over2}}$$
of a recipe. The full recipe calls for
$${3 {1\over4}}$$
cups of flour. How many cups of flour should Kendra use?
b.   Claire walked
$${2{2\over5}}$$
miles. Jason walked
$${{2\over3}}$$
as far as Claire. How many miles did Jason walk?
Problem 3
Solve. Show or explain your work.
a.
$${{2\over3} \times 4{1\over4}}$$
b.
$${3{2\over5} \times {1\over2}}$$
c.
$${{5\over4} \times 3{1\over3}}$$

Problem 1
Act 1: Look at the following recipe -
###IMAGE0###
What do you notice? What do you wonder?
Problem 2
Act 2: Use the following information to determine how many batches of cake can be made, how many servings can be made, and how much of each ingredient are needed to make that much cake -
This is how many eggs there are:
###IMAGE1###
This is how much oil there is:
###IMAGE2###
There are 4 boxes of cake mix.
Water will come from the tap.
Each box of cake makes 12 servings.
Problem 3
Act 3: Reveal the answer –
There is enough of each ingredient to make
$$2\frac{2}{3}$$
boxes, which would produce 32 servings of cake.
To make
$$2\frac23$$
boxes, you need
$$2\frac23$$
cups of water,
$$\frac89$$
cups of vegetable oil, and 8 eggs.
Was your answer reasonable? Why or why not?
Problem 4
Act 4 (the sequel):
Keaton wants to make enough cake so that every student in his fifth grade could have some. There are 100 students in fifth grade. How much more of each ingredient above would Keaton need?

Task
Some of the problems below can be solved by multiplying $\frac18\times\frac25$, while others need a different operation.  Select the ones that can be solved by multiplying these two numbers.  For the remaining, tell what operation is appropriate.  In all cases, solve the problem (if possible) and include appropriate units in the answer.
Two-fifths of the students in Anya’s fifth grade class are girls.  One-eighth of the girls wear glasses.  What fraction of Anya’s class consists of girls who wear glasses?
A farm is in the shape of a rectangle $\frac18$ of a mile long and $\frac25$ of a mile wide.  What is the area of the farm?
There is $\frac25$ of a pizza left.  If Jamie eats another  $\frac18$ of the original whole pizza, what fraction of the original pizza is left over?
In Sam’s fifth grade class, $\frac18$ of the students are boys.  Of those boys, $\frac25$ have red hair.  What fraction of the class is red-haired boys?
Only $\frac{1}{20}$ of the guests at the party wore both red and green.  If $\frac18$ of the guests wore red, what fraction of the guests who wore red also wore green?
Alex was planting a garden. He planted $\frac25$ of the garden with potatoes and $\frac18$ of the garden with lettuce. What fraction of the garden is planted with potatoes or lettuce?
At the start of the trip, the gas tank on the car was $\frac25$ full.  If the trip used $\frac18$ of the remaining gas, what fraction of a tank of gas is left at the end of the trip?
On Monday, $\frac18$ of the students in Mr. Brown’s class were absent from school.  The nurse told Mr. Brown that $\frac25$ of those students who were absent had the flu.  What fraction of the absent students had the flu?
Of the children at Molly’s daycare, $\frac18$ are boys and $\frac25$ of the boys are under 1 year old.  How many boys at the daycare are under one year old?
The track at school is $\frac25$ of a mile long.  If Jason has run $\frac18$ of the way around the track, what fraction of a mile has he run?

problems.text:
Task
The Burj Khalifa (Dubai) is about 2$\frac{1}{2}$ times as tall as the Eiffel Tower (Paris) . The Eiffel Tower is about $\frac{5}{7}$ as tall as the Willis Tower (Chicago).
Which of these buildings is the tallest? Which is the shortest? Explain.
Draw pictures to illustrate.

Problem 1
Act 1: Watch the following video –
The Big Pad (Act: 1)
a.   What do you notice? What do you wonder?
b.   What is the area, in square centimeters, of the big pad? Make an estimate.
Problem 2

The small pad is a square with a length of
$$7\frac{3}{5}$$
cm.
The big pad is also a square and has a length equal to
$$3\frac{2}{3}$$
small pads.
Problem 3

The area of the big pad is
$$776\frac{124}{225}$$
square centimeters. Was your answer reasonable? Why or why not?
Problem 4

Do these dimensions result in the same area as what we computed in Anchor Task #3? Why or why not?

Draw a visual model to represent the solution to the division problem
$${6 \div \frac{2}{3}}$$
.
Then, use your model to explain why this can also be solved with the multiplication problem
$${6 \times \frac{3}{2}}$$

Problem 1
a.   Kyle has 6 Skittles.
$${{1\over3}}$$
of them are red. How many of Kyle's Skittles are red?
b.   Samantha also has 6 Skittles.
$${{2\over3}}$$
of them are red. How many of Samantha’s Skittles are red?
Problem 2
a.   Find the value of each of the following.
###TABLE0###
b.   What do you notice about the solutions to the computations in the leftmost column of the table? What do you wonder?
c.   What do you notice about the solutions in each row of the table? What do you wonder?
Problem 3
a.   Mrs. Pham has 8 apples. She wants to give
$${{3\over4}}$$
of the apples to her students. How many apples will her students get?
b.   In a class of 24 students,
$${{5\over6}}$$
of them are boys. How many boys are in the class?

Problem 1
A small box measures 5 in. across, 8 in. deep, and 6 in. tall. A book that measures 5
$$\times$$
8
$$\times$$
1 in. fits perfectly into the bottom of the box.
###IMAGE0###
a.   What is the volume of one book? How many books will fit into the box? What is the volume of all of the books that will fit into the box?
b.   What is the volume of the box? How is this related to the volume of one book?
c.   A medium box measures 12 inches tall. A different book with dimensions 8
$$\times$$
10
$$\times$$
1 in. fits perfectly into the bottom of the medium box. What is the volume of the medium box?
Problem 2
The same rectangular prism is positioned in two different ways, as shown below. For each prism, use the rectangular face that the prism is “sitting on” as the base of the prism.
###IMAGE1###
For each prism, find the area of the base and multiply by the height. Show that this is the same volume as when all three dimensions are multiplied together.
Problem 3
A rectangular prism has a base area of
$${4{3\over8}}$$
inches
$${^2}$$
, a width of
$$1 \frac{1}{4}$$
inches, and a height of
$${6 {1\over2}}$$
inches. Kirby writes the equation
$$V=4\frac{3}{8}\times1\frac{1}{4}\times6\frac{1}{2}$$
to represent the volume of the prism.
Do you agree with Kirby's equation? Explain and find the volume of the prism.

Problem 1
Solve. Show or explain your work.
a.
$${{2\over3} }$$
of
$${{4\over5}}$$
b.
$${{4\over9}}\times \frac{5}{8}$$
Problem 2
A farmer’s land measures
$${{7\over8}}$$
of a mile long and
$${{4\over5}}$$
of a mile wide. What is the area, in square miles, of the farmer’s land?

Solve. Show or explain your work.
a.
$${{2\over3} \times {5\over6}}$$
b.
$${{4\over9} \times { 3\over 8}}$$

Task
Some of the problems below can be solved by multiplying $\frac18\times\frac25$, while others need a different operation. Select the ones that can be solved by multiplying these two numbers. For the remaining, tell what operation is appropriate. In all cases, solve the problem (if possible) and include appropriate units in the answer.
Two-fifths of the students in Anya’s fifth grade class are girls. One-eighth of the girls wear glasses. What fraction of Anya’s class consists of girls who wear glasses?
A farm is in the shape of a rectangle $\frac18$ of a mile long and $\frac25$ of a mile wide. What is the area of the farm?
A pizza is cut into 8 slices.  There is $\frac25$ of the pizza left. If Jamie eats another slice,  $\frac18$ of the original whole pizza, what fraction of the original pizza is left over?
In Sam’s fifth grade class, $\frac18$ of the students are boys. Of those boys, $\frac25$ have red hair. What fraction of the class is red-haired boys.
Alex was planting a garden. He planted $\frac25$ of the garden with potatoes and $\frac18$ of the garden with lettuce. What fraction of the garden is planted with potatoes or lettuce?

Problem 1

$$\tfrac{2}{5}$$

d.
$$\tfrac{1}{8}$$
of kids in a class are boys. Of those boys,

$$\tfrac{1}{20}$$

$$\tfrac{2}{5}$$

$$\tfrac{1}{8}$$

$$\tfrac{1}{8}$$

$$\tfrac{1}{8}$$
are boys and
$$\tfrac{2}{5}$$

$$\tfrac{2}{5}$$

$${{3\over8}\times{4\over5}}$$
.
Problem 3
One section of a beach has a total of 180 people. Of these 180 people,
$$\frac{4}{9}$$
are wearing a hat and
$$\frac{2}{5}$$
of the people wearing hats are also wearing sunglasses. How many people in that section of beach are wearing both a hat and sunglasses?
a.   Write an expression to determine how many people in that section of the beach are wearing both a hat and sunglasses.
b.   Find the number of people in that section of the beach that are wearing both a hat and sunglasses.
c.   Find the number of people in that section of the beach that are wearing a hat but not wearing sunglasses.

Problem 1
Do the Launch and Pose a Problem sections of SERP's Poster Problem "
No Matter How you Slice It
."
Launch: a. - e.
Pose a Problem: f. - g.
a.   Watch the video on slide 1.
b.   Can you explain how the slicer works?
c.   According to the video, what is the thinnest slice this slicer can make?
d.   If you know the length of a block of cheese, can you determine how many slices it can make?
e.   Suppose you get a new block and you know how thick you want your slices. What do you need to know in order to tell how many sandwiches you can make?
f.   Discuss slides 2, 3, and 4.
g.   Complete Handout #1.
Problem 2
Do the Workshop section of SERP's Poster Problem "
No Matter How You Slice It
", including Handout #2. Students can work in pairs or in small groups.
Handout #2

a.   Make up and solve two of your own slicing problems. In problem A, you should not have any cheese left over, and in problem B, you must have some cheese left over.
b.   For each problem, you need to determine how much cheese you start off with: how long is your block of cheese? You also need to say how thick you want the slices of cheese to be—or you can decide how many slices you will need in total. Keep in mind that the thickness of each slice should be between
$${\frac{1}{32}}$$
and
$${\frac{1}{2}}$$
inches thick.
c.   After you create your problems, make a poster showing each problem and its solution. Each solution should include an explanation, at least one calculation, and a diagram.

Solve the problem with a diagram and explain your answer. Then find the answer using an equation and show that it is the same as what you got in your diagram.

Task
Part 1:
There are two design proposals for a new rectangular park in town.
In design one, $\frac{3}{4}$ of the area of the park is going to be a rectangular grass area and $\frac{1}{2}$ of the grass area will be a rectangular soccer field.
In design two, only $\frac{1}{2}$ of the park is going to be a rectangular grass area and $\frac{3}{4}$ of the grass area will be a rectangular soccer field.
Which design (one or two) will have a bigger soccer field?  Explain your answer. Draw a diagram that can be used to compare the size of the soccer field in the two designs.  Label the values $\frac{1}{2}$ and $\frac{3}{4}$ on the diagram.
Part 2:
Presley and Julia are cutting 1 ft. square poster board to make a sign for the new park. Presley cut her poster so that the length of the top and bottom are $\frac{1}{2}$ ft and the length of the sides are $\frac{3}{4}$ ft. Julia cut her poster so that the lengths of the top and bottom are $\frac{3}{4}$ ft and the length of the sides are $\frac{1}{2}$ ft.
Draw a diagram of each poster board.  Label the values on the diagram.
How are their poster boards similar and different?  Justify your reasoning.

For the remaining, tell what operation is appropriate.  In all cases, solve the problem (if possible) and include appropriate units in the answer.
Two-fifths of the students in Anya’s fifth grade class are girls.  One-eighth of the girls wear glasses.  What fraction of Anya’s class consists of girls who wear glasses?
A farm is in the shape of a rectangle $\frac18$ of a mile long and $\frac25$ of a mile wide.  What is the area of the farm?
There is $\frac25$ of a pizza left.  If Jamie eats another  $\frac18$ of the original whole pizza, what fraction of the original pizza is left over?
In Sam’s fifth grade class, $\frac18$ of the students are boys.  Of those boys, $\frac25$ have red hair.  What fraction of the class is red-haired boys?
Only $\frac{1}{20}$ of the guests at the party wore both red and green.  If $\frac18$ of the guests wore red, what fraction of the guests who wore red also wore green?
Alex was planting a garden. He planted $\frac25$ of the garden with potatoes and $\frac18$ of the garden with lettuce. What fraction of the garden is planted with potatoes or lettuce?
At the start of the trip, the gas tank on the car was $\frac25$ full.  If the trip used $\frac18$ of the remaining gas, what fraction of a tank of gas is left at the end of the trip?
On Monday, $\frac18$ of the students in Mr. Brown’s class were absent from school.  The nurse told Mr. Brown that $\frac25$ of those students who were absent had the flu.  What fraction of the absent students had the flu?
Of the children at Molly’s daycare, $\frac18$ are boys and $\frac25$ of the boys are under 1 year old.  How many boys at the daycare are under one year old?
The track at school is $\frac25$ of a mile long.  If Jason has run $\frac18$ of the way around the track, what fraction of a mile has he run?

IM Commentary
This is the easiest problem in a series of three tasks involving fraction multiplication
that can be solved with pictures or number lines. Students who solve this problem with
a picture or using a number line do not need to subdivide the unit fractions that
comprise 
 to find  of 
 since it is naturally composed of 3 pieces. The next task, 5.NF
Drinking Juice, does require students to subdivide the unit fractions that comprise 
 in
order to find  of 
. The third task, 5.NF Half of a Recipe, also requires subdivision and
involves multiplying a fraction and a mixed number.
Since tasks such as this will be among the first that students see, solutions which
involve (sub)dividing a quantity into equal parts in order to find a fraction of the
quantity should be emphasized. In particular, such solutions should be introduced if
students do not generate them on their own. Students benefit from reasoning through
the solution to such word problems before they are told that they can be solved by
multiplying the fractions; this helps them develop meaning for fraction multiplication.
Also note that the context itself, namely a distance, naturally lends itself to drawing a
tape diagram or number line (both models based on length). The next two tasks in this
series involve other types of quantities, making the number lines in particular more
abstract representations relative to the context.

**Which of the following equations is true?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
An equation is true when the left and right sides of the equal sign have the same value.

Let's compare the values of each side of the equations.

##Is $\dfrac8{10} - \dfrac2{10} = \dfrac{10}{10}$ true?


[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueD{\dfrac8{10}} - \greenD{\dfrac2{10}}$ equals $\maroonD{\dfrac6{10}}$, so the equation is *not* true.

##Is $\dfrac4{12} - \dfrac3{12} = \dfrac2{12}$ true?

$\blueD{\dfrac4{12}} - \greenD{\dfrac3{12}}$ equals $\maroonD{\dfrac1{12}}$, so the equation is *not* true.

##Is $\dfrac42 - \dfrac12 = \dfrac32$ true?



[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueD{\dfrac42} - \greenD{\dfrac12}$ equals $\maroonD{\dfrac32}$, so the equation *is* true.

Each mixed number has a $\blueD{\text{whole number}}$ and a $\maroonD{\text{fraction}}$. Let's start by adding each piece. 

$\blueD9 \maroonD{\dfrac{1}{12}} + \blueD6 \maroonD{\dfrac{7}{12}}$
First, let's add the $\maroonD{\text{fractions}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{9} &\maroonD{\dfrac1{12}}\\\\
+&\blueD{6}&\maroonD{\dfrac7{12}}\\
\hline{}\\
&&\maroonD{\dfrac8{12}}\\
\end{align}$

Next, let's add the $\blueD{\text{whole numbers}}$.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{9} &\maroonD{\dfrac1{12}}\\\\
+&\blueD{6}&\maroonD{\dfrac7{12}}\\
\hline{}\\
&\blueD{15}&\maroonD{\dfrac8{12}}\\
\end{align}$

$9{\dfrac{1}{12}} + 6{\dfrac{7}{12}} = {15} {\dfrac{8}{12}}$

The answer can also be written as ${15}{\dfrac{2}{3}}$ .

items.question_content:
The dolphins at the San Diego zoo eat $3\dfrac18$ buckets of fish each day. The killer whales eat $2\dfrac35$ times as much fish as the dolphins.

**How many buckets of fish do the killer whales eat each day?** 

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] buckets
hints:
The killer whales eat $2\dfrac35$ times the amount of the dolphins' $3\dfrac18$ buckets.

[[☃ image 1]]

To find the amount of buckets the killer whales eat, we multiply: 

$\purpleD{2\dfrac35}\times\blueD{3\dfrac18}$

Now, let's change $\blueD{3\dfrac18}$ into a fraction.

$\begin{align}
\blueD{3\dfrac18} &= \blueD{3} +\blueD{\dfrac18}\\\\
&= \blueD{\dfrac88} + \blueD{\dfrac88} + \blueD{\dfrac88} +\blueD{\dfrac18}\\\\
&= \dfrac{\blueD8+\blueD8+\blueD8+\blueD1}\blueD8\\\\
&=\blueD{\dfrac{25}8}
\end{align}$
The killer whales eat $8\dfrac{1}{8}$ buckets of fish each day.

items.question_content:
Junior's brother is $1\dfrac12$ meters tall.  Junior is $1\dfrac15$ of his brother's height.  

**How tall is Junior?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]  meters
hints:
Junior is $1\dfrac15$ times $1\dfrac12$ meters tall.


[[☃ image 1]]

Let's change $\purpleD{1\dfrac12}$ into a fraction.

$\begin{align}
\purpleD{1\dfrac12} &= \purpleD{1} +\purpleD{\dfrac12}\\\\
&= \purpleD{\dfrac22} +\purpleD{\dfrac12}\\\\
&= \dfrac{\purpleD2+\purpleD1}\purpleD2\\\\
&=\purpleD{\dfrac32}
\end{align}$
Now, let's change $\blueD{1\dfrac15}$ into a fraction.

$\begin{align}
\blueD{1\dfrac15} &= \blueD{1} +\blueD{\dfrac15}\\\\
&= \blueD{\dfrac55}  +\blueD{\dfrac15}\\\\
&= \dfrac{\blueD5+\blueD1}\blueD5\\\\
&=\blueD{\dfrac{6}5}
\end{align}$
$\begin{align}
\purpleD{1\dfrac12} \times \blueD{1\dfrac{1}5} &= \purpleD{\dfrac{3}2} \times \blueD{\dfrac{6}5} \\\\
&= \dfrac{\purpleD{3}\times\blueD{6}}{\purpleD2 \times \blueD5}\\\\
&= \dfrac{\purpleD{3}\times{}\stackrel{\blueD3}{\cancel{\blueD{6}}}  }{ \underset{\purpleD1}{\cancel{\purpleD2}}\times\blueD5 } \\\\
&= \dfrac{9}{5}\\\\
&=1\dfrac{4}{5}
\end{align}$
Junior is $1\dfrac{4}{5}$ meters tall.

$\begin{align}
&\blueD{8} &\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{8}}\\\\
-&\blueD{3}&\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{8}}\\
\hline\end{align}$

We cannot subtract $\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{8}} - \maroonD{\dfrac{3}{8}}$ because $\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{8}}$ is **less** than $\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{8}}$.

We need to regroup $\blueD8\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{8}}$ so that the fractional part is **greater** than $\maroonD{\dfrac{3}{8}}$.



[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align} \blueD8\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{8}}&=\blueD7+\maroonD{1}+ \maroonD{\dfrac{2}{8}}\\\\
&=\blueD7+ \maroonD{\dfrac{8}{8}}+\maroonD{\dfrac{2}{8}}\\\\
&=\blueD7 \maroonD{\dfrac{10}{8}}
\end{align}$
